items.title: Title and abstract
items.description: Identification as a randomised trial in the title; structured summary of trial design, methods, results, and conclusions.

items.title: Background and objectives
items.description: Scientific background and explanation of rationale; specific objectives or hypotheses.

items.title: Trial design
items.description: Description of trial design (e.g. parallel, factorial) including allocation ratio; important changes to methods after trial commencement and reasons.

items.title: Participants
items.description: Eligibility criteria for participants; settings and locations where data were collected.

items.title: Interventions
items.description: Interventions for each group with sufficient details to allow replication, including when and how they were actually administered.

items.title: Outcomes
items.description: Completely defined pre-specified primary and secondary outcomes; any changes to trial outcomes after the trial commenced, with reasons.

items.title: Sample size
items.description: How sample size was determined; when applicable, explanation of any interim analyses and stopping guidelines.

items.title: Randomisation: sequence generation
items.description: Method used to generate the random allocation sequence; type of randomisation (e.g. blocking and block size).

items.title: Randomisation: allocation concealment
items.description: Mechanism used to implement the random allocation sequence, describing any steps taken to conceal the sequence until interventions were assigned.

items.title: Randomisation: implementation
items.description: Who generated the random allocation sequence, who enrolled participants, and who assigned participants to interventions.

items.title: Blinding
items.description: If done, who was blinded after assignment to interventions (e.g. participants, care providers, assessors); description of similarity of interventions.

items.title: Statistical methods
items.description: Statistical methods used to compare groups for primary and secondary outcomes; methods for additional analyses such as subgroup and adjusted analyses.

items.title: Participant flow
items.description: For each group, the numbers of participants who were randomly assigned, received intended treatment, and were analysed for the primary outcome; losses and exclusions after randomisation, with reasons.

items.title: Recruitment
items.description: Dates defining the periods of recruitment and follow-up; why the trial ended or was stopped.

items.title: Baseline data
items.description: A table showing baseline demographic and clinical characteristics for each group.

items.title: Numbers analysed
items.description: For each group, number of participants (denominator) included in each analysis and whether the analysis was by original assigned groups.

items.title: Outcomes and estimation
items.description: For each primary and secondary outcome, results for each group and the estimated effect size and its precision (such as 95% CI); for binary outcomes, presentation of both absolute and relative effect sizes is recommended.

items.title: Ancillary analyses
items.description: Results of any other analyses performed, including subgroup and adjusted analyses, distinguishing pre-specified from exploratory.

items.title: Harms
items.description: All important harms or unintended effects in each group.

items.title: Limitations
items.description: Trial limitations, addressing sources of potential bias, imprecision, and, if relevant, multiplicity of analyses.

items.title: Generalisability
items.description: Generalisability (external validity, applicability) of the trial findings.

items.title: Interpretation
items.description: Interpretation consistent with results, balancing benefits and harms, and considering other relevant evidence.

items.title: Registration
items.description: Registration number and name of trial registry.

items.title: Protocol
items.description: Where the full trial protocol can be accessed, if available.

items.title: Funding
items.description: Sources of funding and other support (such as supply of drugs), and role of funders.
